RICHARD RICHARDS
(1918-1988)

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE

EIGHTEENTH FLOOR
101 CALIFORNIA STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111-5821
TELEPHONE (415) 986-8722

OUR FILE NUMBER

A0480-001

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: City of Avalon Planning Commission
    Member Albert Solomon

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     City of Avalon Planning Commissioner Albert Solomon has
requested that I obtain written advice from the Fair Political
Practices Commission concerning his ability to participate in
various Planning Commission decisions. Commissioner Solomon has
reviewed this letter and agrees that it correctly presents the
facts and the questions he would like answered.

I. Facts

     Commissioner Solomon was appointed to the Avalon
Planning Commission in July, 1990. In 1980, many years prior to
his appointment to the Planning Commission, Commissioner Solomon
entered into a fair market lease of certain improved property
owned by the Santa Catalina Island Company ("SCICo."). The
SCICo. is the largest single owner of land in the City of
Avalon.

     For the past eleven years, Commissioner Solomon has
operated a restaurant, cocktail bar and lounge on the leased
premises. Section 4.1 of the lease (a copy of which is attached
as Exhibit A) provides that Commissioner Solomon will pay to the
SCICo. an annual rent consisting of a base rent, which base rent
is increased each year by the lesser of 7 1/2% of the annual
minimum rent payable during the preceding calendar year or the
increase in a designated CPI index. In addition to the annual
rent, Section 4.2 provides that Commissioner Solomon will pay to

April 30, 1991

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 1, 1991
Page 2

the SCICO. a percentage rent of 6% of gross sales of food plus
10% of all other gross sales.

"Gross sales" are defined in Section 4.2 to include
"all revenues of every kind and nature from any source whatsoever
arising from the use or occupancy of the leased premises. The
lease further provides that "gross sales" "shall not include. . .
revenues from meals furnished to employees when on duty . . ."
(emphasis supplied).

From 1980 through October, 1990, Commissioner Solomon
calculated the employee meal exclusion from gross sales based
upon a flat amount of $13 for each employee on duty.
Commissioner Solomon states that he did so pursuant to an oral
understanding with SCICO.'s former Comptroller Rai Roberts.
Prior to October, 1990, Commissioner Solomon believed that the
SCICO. did not require meal tickets or similar proof of the
employee meals, and Commissioner Solomon did not keep such
records.

In late 1990, the SCICO. conducted an audit.
Following the audit, the SCICO. requested that Commissioner
Solomon provide written proof of the employee meal exclusion for
the preceding three years. Based upon Commissioner Solomon's
inability to produce such documentation, the SCICO. made demand
upon Commissioner Solomon for payment of $52,000, the amount of
percentage rent claimed to be owing for the "improperly" deducted
employee meals, together with interest at the rate of ten (10%)
per annum. A copy of the SCICO. correspondence concerning the
disputed percentage rent is included for your review as Exhibit B.
Commissioner Solomon denies that the exclusion of employee
meals was improper and denies that any amount is due to the
SCICO.

Commissioner Solomon does not concede the validity of
the claim by the SCICO. and he denies that his refusal to pay the
amount claimed by the SCICO. stems from any financial
consideration. The SCICO. has apparently determined that it will
not seek to enforce its reading of the lease provision through
litigation.

The SCICO. also stated in its letter that it was
"terminating all future discussions regarding your proposed
development of El Encanto into a hotel." The letter notes that
Commissioner Solomon had last held a very preliminary discussion
concerning the El Encanto property six months prior to the SCICO.
dispute with Commissioner Solomon over the percentage rent at Solomon's landing. Commissioner Solomon
has not engaged in negotiations with the SCICO. for this property
since before his appointment to the Planning Commission.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 1, 1991
Page 3

III.  Questions Presented.

1.    Does Commissioner Solomon's resolution of the disputed claim
      constitute "income" for purposes of the Political Reform
      Act?

2.    To what extent must Commissioner Solomon abstain from
      participating in or voting upon matters having a material
      financial effect on the SCICo.?

      Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any
questions or need any additional facts.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Pamela Albers


PAA:paa
0840791
CC:  Albert Solomon

sections.facts:
Commissioner Solomon was appointed to the Avalon Planning Commission in July, 1990. In 1980, many years prior to his appointment to the Planning Commission, Commissioner Solomon entered into a fair market lease of certain improved property owned by the Santa Catalina Island Company ("SCICo."). The SCICo. is the largest single owner of land in the City of Avalon.

For the past eleven years, Commissioner Solomon has operated a restaurant, cocktail bar and lounge on the leased premises. Section 4.1 of the lease (a copy of which is attached as Exhibit A) provides that Commissioner Solomon will pay to the SCICo. an annual rent consisting of a base rent, which base rent is increased each year by the lesser of 7 1/2% of the annual minimum rent payable during the preceding calendar year or the increase in a designated CPI index. In addition to the annual rent, Section 4.2 provides that Commissioner Solomon will pay to

the SCICo. a percentage rent of 6% of gross sales of food plus 10% of all other gross sales.

"Gross sales" are defined in Section 4.2 to include "all revenues of every kind and nature from any source whatsoever arising from the use or occupancy of the leased premises. The lease further provides that "gross sales" "shall not include... revenues from meals furnished to employees when on duty..." (emphasis supplied).

From 1980 through October, 1990, Commissioner Solomon calculated the employee meal exclusion from gross sales based upon a flat amount of $13 for each employee on duty. Commissioner Solomon states that he did so pursuant to an oral understanding with SCICo.'s former Comptroller Rai Roberts. Prior to October, 1990, Commissioner Solomon believed that the SCICo. did not require meal tickets or similar proof of the employee meals, and Commissioner Solomon did not keep such records.

In late 1990, the SCICO. conducted an audit. Following the audit, the SCICo. requested that Commissioner Solomon provide written proof of the employee meal exclusion for the preceding three years. Based upon Commissioner Solomon's inability to produce such documentation, the SCICo. made demand upon Commissioner Solomon for payment of $52,000, the amount of percentage rent claimed to be owing for the "improperly" deducted employee meals, together with interest at the rate of ten (10%) per annum. A copy of the SCICo. correspondence concerning the disputed percentage rent is included for your review as Exhibit B. Commissioner Solomon denies that the exclusion of employee meals was improper and denies that any amount is due to the SCICo.

Commissioner Solomon does not concede the validity of the claim by the SCICo. and he denies that his refusal to pay the amount claimed by the SCICo. stems from any financial consideration. The SCICo. has apparently determined that it will not seek to enforce its reading of the lease provision through litigation.

The SCICo. also stated in its letter that it was "terminating all future discussions regarding your proposed development of El Encanto into a hotel." The letter notes that the SCICo. and Commissioner Solomon had last held a very preliminary discussion concerning the El Encanto property six months prior to the SCICo. dispute with Commissioner Solomon over the percentage rent at Solomon's landing. Commissioner Solomon has not engaged in negotiations with the SCICo. for this property since before his appointment to the Planning Commission.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 1, 1991

II. Questions Presented.

1. Does Commissioner Solomon's resolution of the disputed claim constitute "income" for purposes of the Political Reform Act?

2. To what extent must Commissioner Solomon abstain from participating in or voting upon matters having a material financial effect on the SCICo.?
